symptoms: Rectangular gray-tan lesions parallel to leaf veins
Tan/gray center with dark brown borders
Entire leaf death in severe cases
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot) is a fungal disease of corn that causes rectangular gray-tan lesions parallel to the leaf veins. Severe infections can significantly reduce yield and plant vigor.

diseases.details: Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot) is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis. The leaves show rectangular gray-tan lesions parallel to leaf veins and tan/gray centers with dark brown borders. Under favorable conditions, lesions may expand or merge, leading to significant damage to foliage. Severe infections can significantly reduce crop yields.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). However, insect feeding wounds can create entry points that allow pathogens to infect the plant.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to the spread of Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). Excess moisture on leaves creates a favorable environment for the fungus. Overhead irrigation splashes pathogens between plants, increasing disease spread. Watering at the base of the plant or using drip irrigation helps control the disease.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). This disease typically requires warm, moist conditions to spread, so dry conditions do not favor it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). Poor drainage or infected crop residue in the soil can harbor the fungus and lead to infection. Improving field drainage and removing infected debris can help reduce disease risk.

sunlight.details: Sunlight levels can influence Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). Dense or shaded canopies increase humidity, which favors fungal growth. Adequate sunlight and proper plant spacing improve airflow and help reduce disease risk.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot). However, balanced fertilization helps maintain plant vigor and resistance. Healthy, well-nourished plants can better tolerate disease pressure.

generalStress.details: Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot) can place significant stress on the plant. It can reduce leaf area and photosynthesis. If not managed promptly, severe infections may lead to reduced yield and plant vigor.

treatment: Fungicide application (e.g., azoxystrobin, tebuconazole) at first symptoms
Resistant hybrids (e.g., varieties with GLT resistance)
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops (e.g., soybeans)
Deep plowing to bury residue
Use disease-free seed

expert_resources.title: Gray leaf spot of corn (UC IPM)

symptoms: Small round to elongated brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules erupt through epidermis
In severe cases, leaves appear rusty and necrotic
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Common Rust is a fungal disease of corn that causes small, reddish-brown pustules on leaves. Severe rust can significantly reduce photosynthesis and yield under favorable conditions.

diseases.details: Common Rust is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. The leaves show small round to elongated reddish-brown pustules on both surfaces. Under humid conditions, pustules may break open and spread spores to other leaves. Severe infections can reduce photosynthesis and lower yield.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Common Rust. However, insect feeding wounds can create entry points that allow pathogens to infect the plant.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to the spread of Common Rust. Excess leaf wetness and dew favor spore germination. Overhead irrigation can splash spores onto new leaves. Watering at the base or using drip irrigation can help reduce leaf wetness and disease spread.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Common Rust. Rust diseases develop in cool, moist environments; dry conditions do not favor rust development.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Common Rust. The pathogen spreads by windborne spores between corn plants, not through the soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Common Rust. However, adequate sunlight and good air circulation can help foliage dry faster and reduce rust development.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Common Rust. However, well-nourished plants are generally more vigorous and better able to resist disease.

generalStress.details: Common Rust can put stress on the plant by reducing its photosynthetic area. Heavy rust infection can lead to premature leaf death. If the disease spreads unchecked, yield losses may result.

treatment: Fungicide application (e.g., chlorothalonil) if early and severe
Use resistant hybrids (e.g., with puccinia rust resistance)
Remove volunteer corn and weeds that can harbor rust
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops
Avoid planting in cool, wet conditions if possible

expert_resources.title: Common Rust of Corn (Kansas State Univ)

symptoms: Long elliptical tan lesions parallel to leaf veins
Lesions initially narrow, then expand and overlap
Severe defoliation in susceptible hybrids
causes: Cool to moderate temperatures (68-80°F) with high humidity
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Northern Leaf Blight is a fungal disease of corn that causes long tan lesions on leaves. Severe infections can cause large areas of leaf blight and reduce yield.

diseases.details: Northern Leaf Blight is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Exserohilum turcicum. The leaves show long elliptical tan lesions parallel to the veins. Lesions can expand and merge, causing large dead areas on leaves. Severe infections may lead to premature leaf death and significant yield losses.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Northern Leaf Blight. However, insect feeding wounds can create entry points that allow pathogens to infect the plant.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to the spread of Northern Leaf Blight. Excess leaf wetness and humidity favor the fungus. Overhead irrigation can splash spores onto healthy leaves. Watering at the base or using drip irrigation helps keep foliage dry and reduces disease spread.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Northern Leaf Blight. This disease requires moisture to infect; dry conditions do not promote it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Northern Leaf Blight. The fungus overwinters in infected corn residue in the field. Good crop rotation and tillage to bury residues can help reduce the inoculum in soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Northern Leaf Blight. However, adequate sunlight and plant spacing improve airflow and can help foliage dry faster, reducing disease risk.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Northern Leaf Blight. However, balanced fertilization supports plant health and may help plants better tolerate infection.

generalStress.details: Northern Leaf Blight can place stress on the plant by reducing leaf area for photosynthesis. Severe infections may cause defoliation, weakening the plant. If not managed, significant yield loss can occur.

treatment: Fungicide application (e.g., propiconazole) at early tassel stage
Plant resistant hybrids when available
Use tillage or crop rotation to reduce residue
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops (e.g., soybean)
Delay planting in areas with history of blight

diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Corn plant appears healthy with no symptoms of disease.

diseases.details: No disease was detected. The plant appears healthy with no symptoms.

pests.details: Pests are not causing any disease in this plant. Insect feeding wounds can create entry points, but currently no infection is present.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Good watering practices should still be maintained to keep the plant healthy.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Keep consistent soil moisture for healthy growth.

soil.details: Soil conditions are not causing disease here. Good soil drainage and fertility should be maintained.

sunlight.details: Sunlight is not causing any issue. The plant should receive adequate sunlight for healthy growth.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency is not causing disease. Balanced fertilization should be continued for optimal health.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under stress from any disease. Continue good care practices.

symptoms: Small purple spots on leaves and shoots, enlarging to tan or black lesions
Dark brown 'M'-shaped lesions on green stems
Shriveled black mummies on berries
causes: Warm (70-90°F) and humid conditions during bloom
Spores overwinter in mummified fruit and leaf debris
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Black Rot is a serious fungal disease of grapes. It causes dark lesions on leaves and fruit, eventually blackening and mummifying the grapes, which can lead to severe yield loss.

diseases.details: Black Rot is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwellii. Leaves show small purple spots that enlarge into tan centers with reddish-black margins. Infected berries shrivel and turn into hard black 'mummies'. Under warm, humid conditions during bloom, the disease can spread quickly and ruin clusters.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Black Rot. However, insect feeding wounds can create entry points that allow pathogens like this fungus to infect plant tissue.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Black Rot. Excess moisture and wet foliage create an ideal environment for the fungus. Water on leaves helps spores splash to new sites. Watering at the base or using drip irrigation reduces leaf wetness and helps control the disease.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Black Rot. This disease requires warm, moist conditions to develop, so dry conditions do not favor it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Black Rot. The fungus overwinters in mummified fruit and leaf debris in the vineyard floor. Removing and destroying old debris reduces inoculum for next season.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Black Rot. However, good air circulation and sunlight help foliage dry faster, which reduces the humidity the fungus needs.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Black Rot. However, healthy plants with balanced nutrition are generally better able to resist infection.

generalStress.details: Black Rot can stress the grapevine by destroying fruit and foliage. Severe infections can significantly reduce yield and vine vigor if not treated.

treatment: Apply fungicides (e.g., captan, myclobutanil) at bloom and early fruit set
Prune out and destroy infected shoots and mummies in winter
Maintain canopy open for air movement
prevention: Remove dropped fruit and leaf litter
Use resistant grape varieties when available

symptoms: Tiger-stripe leaf pattern with yellow and brown discolouration
Berries show dark spots (Black Measles pattern)
Sudden vine collapse in severe cases
causes: Fungal complex: Phaeomoniella chlamydospora + Phaeoacremonium spp.

diseases.details: Esca (Black Measles) is present on this plant. Dark spots and stripes appear on the leaves, and grapes may shrivel or crack. This is a serious disease that lives inside the wood of the vine and is difficult to cure once established.

pests.details: Pests do not cause Esca. This disease is caused by a group of fungal germs that enter through pruning wounds.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause Esca. This disease enters through wounds in the vine wood, not through water on the leaves.

underwatering.details: Underwatering can contribute to Esca. Water stress weakens the vine and makes it less able to fight back against the disease. Keep your grapevines watered consistently, especially in dry periods.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not directly cause Esca. This disease lives in the wood of old vines and spreads through pruning wounds.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not cause Esca. Normal sunlight is good for the vine and helps it stay strong.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Esca. A well-fed vine is better at coping with the disease, but nutrients are not the root cause.

generalStress.details: This disease is putting serious stress on the plant. The disease is inside the vine wood and cannot be fully removed. If not managed, the vine will slowly decline and produce less fruit each year.

treatment: No chemical cure — paint all pruning wounds immediately with fungicidal wound sealant
Remove and burn severely infected vines
Minimise pruning wounds — use double pruning technique
prevention: Apply Trichoderma-based wound protectant after every pruning cut
Prune during dry weather when wound healing is fastest
Avoid large pruning wounds
Maintain balanced nutrition to reduce vine stress
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Esca (Black Measles) is an issue affecting Grape. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Irregular brown necrotic lesions on older leaves
Lesions with diffuse margins, often coalescing
Premature defoliation in severe cases
causes: Warm humid conditions late in season

diseases.details: Leaf Blight is present on this plant. Dark brown spots appear on older leaves and the edges begin to dry and die. In severe cases leaves fall off early and the plant loses energy needed for fruit production.

pests.details: Pests do not cause Grape Leaf Blight. This is a fungal disease and is not started by insects.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Grape Leaf Blight. Wet leaves and high humidity help the fungus grow and spread. Water at the base only and avoid wetting leaves during watering.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Grape Leaf Blight. The disease needs wet, humid conditions to spread — dry conditions are not a risk factor.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not directly cause Grape Leaf Blight. The fungus lives and spreads on the leaf surface, not in the soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not cause Grape Leaf Blight. Good sunlight and air circulation actually help reduce the spread of this disease.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Grape Leaf Blight. Keep your vines well-fed for faster recovery after treatment.

generalStress.details: This disease is putting stress on the plant. Early leaf drop reduces the plant's energy and can weaken grape production. Treat quickly before more leaves are lost.

treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide or Mancozeb at first signs
Remove and dispose of infected fallen leaves
Improve canopy ventilation with targeted pruning
prevention: Maintain open canopy through summer pruning
Avoid late-season overhead irrigation
Remove leaf debris at end of season
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Leaf Blight (Isariopsis Leaf Spot) is an issue affecting Grape. Proper management is required.

diseases.details: No disease detected. Your grapevine looks healthy and the leaves look clean.

pests.details: No pest damage visible. Check under the leaves occasionally for any new insect activity.

overwatering.details: No signs of overwatering. Continue watering at the base of the vine to keep roots healthy.

underwatering.details: No signs of underwatering. The vine looks well-watered and is growing normally.

soil.details: Soil conditions look fine. Good soil drainage is important for grapevines — make sure water does not pool around the base.

sunlight.details: The vine is getting good sunlight. Continue as normal and prune regularly to keep air flowing through the canopy.

nutrientDeficiency.details: No signs of nutrient shortage. Keep up fertilizing through the growing season for strong fruit production.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under any stress. Keep monitoring every few days to catch problems early.

prevention: Apply preventive fungicide program starting at budbreak
Maintain open canopy for air circulation
Scout weekly for pests and disease
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Healthy is an issue affecting Grape. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Yellow shoots or mottled leaves, often with asymmetrical coloration
Misshapen, bitter fruit
Twigs and branches may die back
causes: Spread by Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri)
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Huanglongbing (Citrus Greening) is a bacterial disease of citrus that causes yellowing of leaves and bitter, misshapen fruit. Infected trees decline and produce unmarketable fruit.

diseases.details: Huanglongbing (Citrus Greening) is present on this plant. It is caused by a phloem-inhabiting bacterium (Liberibacter) transmitted by the Asian citrus psyllid. Leaves show yellow mottling, often asymmetrically on each leaf. Fruit are small, lopsided, and bitter. Over time, infected trees decline in vigor and dieback.

pests.details: Pest damage can contribute to Huanglongbing (Citrus Greening). The Asian citrus psyllid feeds on the plant and spreads the bacteria from tree to tree. Insect feeding wounds also provide entry points for pathogens.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause Huanglongbing. This disease is spread by an insect vector rather than irrigation.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Huanglongbing. However, drought stress can weaken trees and make symptoms appear more severe.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Huanglongbing. The bacterium is spread by insects, not by soil pathogens.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Huanglongbing. Healthy trees should still receive full sun for optimal growth.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Huanglongbing. However, balanced fertilization supports tree health and may help infected trees sustain yields longer.

generalStress.details: Huanglongbing places serious stress on the tree. It reduces leaf function and fruit production. If not managed (by controlling vectors), infected trees will decline and yield losses will be severe.

treatment: Remove infected trees (no cure for HLB)
Control Asian citrus psyllid with insecticides
Use HLB-tolerant rootstocks if available
prevention: Monitor and control psyllid vector populations
Use certified disease-free nursery trees

symptoms: Small brown lesions on leaves surrounded by yellow halos
Fruits develop dark scabs
Leaves may defoliate under heavy infection
causes: Spread by rain splash and contaminated tools
Warm (75-86°F) and wet conditions favor spread
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Bacterial Spot is a bacterial disease of bell peppers that causes small brown spots with yellow halos on leaves and fruit scabs. Severe infections can lead to defoliation and crop loss.

diseases.details: Bacterial Spot is present on this plant. It is caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria. Leaves show small brown water-soaked lesions often surrounded by yellow halos. Fruits develop dark, raised scabs. Under warm, humid conditions, the bacteria can spread rapidly to nearby plants.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Bacterial Spot. However, insect feeding wounds can allow the bacteria to enter the leaf tissue.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Bacterial Spot. Excess moisture on leaves creates a favorable environment for the bacteria. Water splash from overhead irrigation can carry bacteria between plants. Watering at the base or using drip irrigation helps reduce leaf wetness and disease spread.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Bacterial Spot. This disease actually needs warm, wet conditions to spread, so drought stress does not trigger it.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Bacterial Spot. The bacterium spreads through water and infected plant material, not through the soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Bacterial Spot. However, healthy plants still need full sunlight for best growth.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Bacterial Spot. However, good fertilization supports plant health and may help plants better resist stress.

generalStress.details: Bacterial Spot can place stress on the plant by reducing leaf area and fruit quality. Severe infections can cause defoliation and unmarketable fruit if not managed.

treatment: Copper-based bactericides can reduce spread
Use disease-free seed and sanitation
Remove affected leaves and avoid overhead watering
prevention: Rotate crops away from peppers
Plant resistant pepper varieties if available

diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Bell pepper plant appears healthy with no symptoms of disease.

diseases.details: No disease was detected. The plant appears healthy with no symptoms.

pests.details: Pests are not causing any disease in this plant. Good pest management should be maintained to keep the plant healthy.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Proper watering at the base keeps the plant healthy.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Consistent soil moisture is important for growth.

soil.details: Soil conditions are not causing disease here. Maintain proper soil fertility and drainage.

sunlight.details: Sunlight is not an issue. The plant should continue receiving adequate sunlight.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency is not causing problems. Keep fertilization balanced for healthy growth.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under stress from any disease. Continue good care practices.

symptoms: Irregular brown to black spots with concentric rings on lower leaves
Leaves yellow and drop
Fruit and stem lesions
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Early Blight is a common fungal disease of potato that causes leaf spots with target-like rings. Severe infections can cause defoliation and reduce tuber yield.

diseases.details: Early Blight is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. The lower leaves have irregular brown to black spots often with concentric rings. Infected leaves yellow and die. Under warm, humid conditions, lesions expand quickly and may cause extensive defoliation.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Early Blight. However, insect damage can create wounds that allow fungal pathogens to enter the tissue.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Early Blight. Excess soil moisture and humidity favor fungal growth. Water splash can move spores to lower leaves. Watering carefully at soil level and avoiding water on foliage can reduce spread.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Early Blight. This disease typically requires warm, moist conditions to develop, so drought stress does not trigger it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Early Blight. The fungus survives in cull piles and soil from previous crops. Good crop rotation and sanitation of infected debris help prevent disease carryover.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Early Blight. However, good sunlight and air circulation help foliage dry faster and can limit disease severity.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Early Blight. However, well-fertilized plants may grow more vigorously and better tolerate infection.

generalStress.details: Early Blight can stress the potato plant by destroying leaf area. Severe infections can significantly reduce photosynthesis, leading to smaller tubers and yield loss.

treatment: Apply fungicides (e.g., chlorothalonil) on a regular schedule
Remove volunteer potatoes and crop debris
Plant resistant varieties if available
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops (e.g., corn, legumes)
Use certified disease-free seed potatoes

symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems
White fuzzy sporulation on leaf undersides in humid weather
Tubers develop brown rot under the skin
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Late Blight is a devastating disease of potato and tomato. It causes large dark lesions on leaves and fuzzy growth on the underside. Infected tubers rot in storage, and crops can be wiped out rapidly in cool, wet conditions.

diseases.details: Late Blight is present on this plant. It is caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Leaves and stems show large water-soaked lesions that turn dark brown or black. In humid weather, white fuzzy sporulation appears on the undersides of leaves. Tubers and fruits become infected, turning brown and rotting. The disease can spread rapidly under cool, wet conditions.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Late Blight. However, insect wounds can create entry points that allow the pathogen to infect the plant.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Late Blight. Excess moisture and humidity create ideal conditions for the pathogen. Overhead irrigation can increase leaf wetness. Using drip irrigation and avoiding water on foliage helps minimize risk.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Late Blight. The disease actually needs abundant moisture to spread; dry conditions are not conducive to infection.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Late Blight. The pathogen can survive in infected tubers left in the soil or cull piles. Removing volunteer potatoes and infected debris prevents overwintering of the pathogen.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Late Blight. However, open sunny canopies help leaves dry faster and may reduce disease severity.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Late Blight. However, balanced fertilization improves plant health, which can help resist stress.

generalStress.details: Late Blight causes severe stress on the plant by destroying foliage and tubers. Rapid spread can lead to complete crop failure.

treatment: Apply appropriate fungicides (e.g., chlorothalonil, mancozeb) at first signs
Destroy infected plants immediately
Ensure good field sanitation
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed
Avoid overhead irrigation

diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Potato plant appears healthy with no symptoms of disease.

diseases.details: No disease was detected. The plant appears healthy with no symptoms.

pests.details: Pests are not causing any disease. Continue monitoring, as insect wounds can later allow infections.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Maintain proper soil moisture to keep it healthy.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause disease in this plant. Keep the soil evenly moist for best growth.

soil.details: Soil conditions are not causing disease here. Good drainage and fertility should be maintained.

sunlight.details: Sunlight is not an issue. The plant should continue receiving adequate sunlight.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency is not causing problems. Balanced nutrition is important for continued plant health.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under stress from any disease. Continue good care practices.

diseases.details: No disease detected. Your soybean plant looks healthy and is growing well.

pests.details: No pest damage visible. Check the leaves and pods regularly for any insect activity.

overwatering.details: No signs of overwatering. Soybeans need consistent moisture but also good drainage — continue as normal.

underwatering.details: No signs of underwatering. The plant looks well-watered and is in good shape.

soil.details: Soil conditions look fine. Good soil health is important for soybeans — rotate crops every 2-3 years to keep the soil productive.

sunlight.details: The plant is getting enough sunlight. Soybeans need full sun for good pod development — continue as normal.

nutrientDeficiency.details: No signs of nutrient shortage. Soybeans can get nitrogen from the soil naturally — keep up regular fertilizing for other nutrients.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under any stress. Keep monitoring every 2-3 days to catch any problems early.

prevention: Use certified disease-free seed with appropriate seed treatments
Monitor for sudden death syndrome during wet periods
Ensure proper nodulation for nitrogen fixation
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Healthy is an issue affecting Soybean. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Irregular purple spots on upper leaf surface
Spots enlarge, centers turn brown-gray
Severe cases cause leaf edges to look scorched
Entire leaf death in heavy infections

diseases.details: Leaf Scorch is present on this plant. Small dark purple spots appear on the upper side of leaves, and the edges of the leaves begin to turn brown and dry out. Severely infected leaves die and fall off, reducing the plant's energy and fruit production.

pests.details: Pests do not cause Leaf Scorch. This is a fungal disease and is not spread by insects.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Leaf Scorch. Wet leaves and high humidity help the fungus spread. Water only at the base of the plant and avoid getting the leaves wet.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Leaf Scorch. The disease needs moisture on leaf surfaces to spread — dry conditions are not a risk factor.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Leaf Scorch. The fungus survives in infected plant debris in the soil and on old leaves. Remove and destroy all old leaves around the plant to reduce the spread.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not cause Leaf Scorch. Good sunlight and air circulation actually help keep the leaves drier and reduce fungal spread.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Leaf Scorch. Well-fed plants recover better — continue fertilizing through treatment.

generalStress.details: This disease is putting stress on the plant. Leaf loss reduces the plant's energy and can lower your strawberry harvest. Act quickly to prevent more leaves from being infected.

treatment: Apply Captan or Myclobutanil fungicide
Remove and destroy infected leaves from the field
Renovate (mow and thin) the planting after harvest
prevention: Switch to drip irrigation — keep foliage dry
Remove infected leaf debris after harvest
Renovate planting annually after fruiting season
Plant resistant varieties (Allstar, Honeoye)

expert_resources.title: Strawberry Diseases in Minnesota — UMN Extension

diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Leaf Scorch is an issue affecting Strawberry. Proper management is required.

diseases.details: No disease detected. Your strawberry plant looks healthy and the leaves look clean.

pests.details: No pest damage visible. Check under the leaves for tiny spider mites or aphids regularly.

overwatering.details: No signs of overwatering. Continue watering at the base to keep roots healthy and leaves dry.

underwatering.details: No signs of underwatering. The plant looks well-watered and is growing normally.

soil.details: Soil conditions look fine. Remove old and dead leaves regularly to keep the area around the plant clean.

sunlight.details: The plant is getting enough sunlight. Strawberries need full sun for good fruit production — continue as normal.

nutrientDeficiency.details: No signs of nutrient shortage. Keep fertilizing for strong fruit development.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under any stress. Keep monitoring every 2-3 days to catch any problems early.

prevention: Renovate planting after harvest season
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Apply preventive fungicide during flowering
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Healthy is an issue affecting Strawberry. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Small water-soaked spots turning dark brown with yellow halos
Raised scab-like lesions on fruit
Severe defoliation under humid conditions
causes: Warm 75-86F with rain and overhead irrigation

diseases.details: Bacterial Spot is present on this plant. Small dark spots with yellow edges appear on the leaves, and raised rough spots form on the fruits. It spreads quickly through water splashing and can ruin the fruit harvest.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Bacterial Spot. However, insect bite wounds on leaves can let the bacteria enter the plant more easily.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Bacterial Spot. Splashing water moves bacteria from leaf to leaf very easily. Switch to drip irrigation or water only at the base of the plant to reduce spreading.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Bacterial Spot. The bacteria spreads through water and wet conditions — drought does not make this disease worse.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Bacterial Spot. This disease spreads mainly through infected seeds and water splashing, not through the soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not cause Bacterial Spot. Good sunlight helps dry out the leaves and slow the bacteria from spreading.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Bacterial Spot. A well-fed plant recovers better from this disease — continue fertilizing normally.

generalStress.details: This disease is putting serious stress on the plant. Infected fruits develop rough spots and cannot be sold. If not treated within 1-2 weeks, the disease will spread further and more fruit will be lost.

treatment: Apply copper bactericide (copper hydroxide) + Mancozeb immediately
Spray every 5-7 days during warm wet weather
Remove heavily infected lower leaves
Avoid working in wet fields — bacteria spread on hands and tools
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants and seed
Install drip irrigation — eliminate overhead watering
Rotate crops — avoid tomatoes and peppers in same field for 2 years
Apply copper spray preventively at transplanting

expert_resources.title: Bacterial Spot of Pepper and Tomato — NC State Extension

diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Bacterial Spot is an issue affecting Tomato. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Dark brown concentric rings on lower leaves (target-like spots)
Leaves yellow and drop
Circular dark lesions on fruit
causes: Survives in soil and plant debris
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Early Blight is a common fungal disease of tomato that causes concentric ring spots on leaves and dark lesions on fruit. Severe infections can defoliate plants and reduce yield.

diseases.details: Early Blight is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. The lower leaves show dark brown spots with concentric rings. Infected leaves may yellow and fall off. Infected fruit develop brown, scab-like lesions. The disease thrives in warm, humid conditions.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Early Blight. However, insect wounds can provide entry points for fungi like this one.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Early Blight. Excess moisture and humidity in the canopy favor the fungus. Water splash can spread spores to healthy leaves. Watering at the base and avoiding wetting foliage can reduce spread.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Early Blight. The fungus needs moisture to infect; dry conditions do not trigger it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Early Blight. The fungus survives in soil and crop debris. Removing plant debris and rotating crops helps reduce the inoculum.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Early Blight. Adequate sunlight and spacing help leaves dry faster, which can reduce disease severity.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Early Blight. Balanced nutrition helps keep plants vigorous and better able to resist stress.

generalStress.details: Early Blight places stress on the plant by destroying foliage. Severe infection can significantly reduce the plant’s ability to produce fruit.

treatment: Apply fungicides (e.g., chlorothalonil, copper) early
Remove lower leaves and debris
Mulch around base to prevent soil splash
prevention: Rotate with non-tomato crops
Plant resistant varieties (if available)

symptoms: Water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown or black
White fuzzy growth on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark lesions on stems and green fruit
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Late Blight is a serious disease of tomato (and potato) that causes large dark spots on foliage and fruit. In wet conditions, white moldy growth appears. The disease can rapidly destroy tomato crops.

diseases.details: Late Blight is present on this plant. It is caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Leaves have water-soaked lesions that rapidly turn brown. In humid weather, white fuzzy sporulation forms on leaf undersides. Stems and green fruit also get dark lesions. This disease spreads quickly in cool, wet weather.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Late Blight. However, feeding injuries can allow the pathogen to infect the plant more easily.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Late Blight. Waterlogged soil and wet foliage create ideal conditions for the pathogen. Drip irrigation and avoiding overhead watering help minimize leaf wetness.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Late Blight. The pathogen requires moisture to infect; dry conditions do not promote it.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Late Blight. The pathogen can overwinter in tubers or plant debris left in the field. Removing crop debris and planting healthy seed reduce risk.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Late Blight. Adequate sunlight exposure helps plants stay healthy and dry out faster.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Late Blight. However, proper nutrition supports plant health, which can help tolerate stress better.

generalStress.details: Late Blight causes severe stress by destroying foliage and fruit. Infected plants collapse quickly, leading to total loss if unchecked.

treatment: Apply appropriate fungicides (e.g., chlorothalonil) at first signs
Remove and destroy infected plants
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants
Avoid overhead irrigation

symptoms: Yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces
Fuzzy gray to tan mold on undersides of leaves
Leaves eventually turn brown and die
causes: Poor air circulation in greenhouse/crowded plantings
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Leaf Mold is a fungal disease of tomato that causes yellow patches on leaves and grayish mold underneath. Infected foliage quickly dies, leading to defoliation.

diseases.details: Leaf Mold is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Fulvia fulva. The upper surfaces of leaves have yellow spots, and the undersides develop a dense gray mold. Infected leaves turn brown and fall off. The disease thrives in high humidity with poor airflow.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Leaf Mold. However, insect damage can create entry points, though Leaf Mold primarily relies on humidity.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Leaf Mold. Excess moisture and humidity around plants create ideal conditions for the fungus. Improving airflow and watering at the base help reduce leaf wetness.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Leaf Mold. The pathogen actually needs high humidity; dry conditions limit its growth.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Leaf Mold. The fungus lives on leaf surfaces and thrives in humid air, not in soil.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Leaf Mold. Adequate light and spacing help plants dry and reduce humidity.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Leaf Mold. However, healthy nutrition ensures vigorous growth that can better withstand stress.

generalStress.details: Leaf Mold stresses the plant by destroying foliage. Severe infections lead to defoliation, reducing yield and plant vigor.

treatment: Use fungicidal sprays (e.g., chlorothalonil) early
Remove infected leaves
Increase greenhouse ventilation and reduce humidity
prevention: Space plants for good air flow
Avoid overhead watering

symptoms: Numerous small circular tan spots with dark edges on older leaves
White centers in advanced lesions
Lower leaves may turn yellow and drop
causes: Spores splash from soil or rain to foliage
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Septoria Leaf Spot is a fungal disease of tomato that causes many small round spots on older leaves. Severe infections can defoliate the plant and weaken it.

diseases.details: Septoria Leaf Spot is present on this plant. It is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. The older leaves show numerous small tan spots with dark brown margins, often with white centers. Infected leaves turn yellow and fall. The fungus spreads via rain splash in warm, humid weather.

pests.details: Pests are not causing Septoria Leaf Spot. However, insects can create wounds that allow pathogens to enter more easily.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Septoria Leaf Spot. Leaf wetness from overhead irrigation or rain helps spores germinate and spread. Watering at the soil level and mulching can reduce splash.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Septoria Leaf Spot. The pathogen actually requires moisture; dry conditions limit its development.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Septoria Leaf Spot. The fungus can survive on infected debris in the soil. Removing and destroying old foliage helps prevent spread.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Septoria Leaf Spot. However, adequate sunlight and spacing improve drying and reduce disease risk.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Septoria Leaf Spot. Balanced nutrition ensures healthy foliage that can tolerate stress better.

generalStress.details: Septoria Leaf Spot stresses the plant by defoliating the foliage. Severe infection can greatly reduce yield if not managed.

treatment: Apply fungicides (e.g., chlorothalonil, copper) regularly
Prune lower leaves and remove debris
Improve soil drainage and mulching
prevention: Space plants to increase air flow
Rotate with non-tomato crops

symptoms: Stippled yellow or bronzed foliage
Fine webbing on undersides of leaves
Leaves eventually turn brown and drop
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Spider mites are tiny pests that suck sap from tomato leaves, causing yellow stippling and webbing. Severe infestations can defoliate plants.

diseases.details: No disease was detected on this plant. The foliage damage is caused by pests (spider mites), not a pathogen.

pests.details: Pest damage is contributing to this problem. Two-spotted spider mites are feeding on the leaves and creating fine webbing. Their feeding causes yellow stippling and leaf browning.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause spider mite infestations. Actually, spider mites often thrive when plants are under drought stress and high heat.

underwatering.details: Underwatering can contribute to spider mite problems. Drought-stressed plants are more vulnerable to mite infestation and damage.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not directly cause spider mites. However, poor soil moisture can stress plants, making them more susceptible.

sunlight.details: Sunlight levels can influence spider mite pressure. Hot, sunny conditions favor mite reproduction and spread.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not directly cause spider mites. However, healthy nutrition can help plants recover from mite damage.

generalStress.details: Spider mite infestation is stressing the plant significantly. It is causing leaf damage and reduced vigor. If not controlled quickly, the plant may lose most of its foliage.

treatment: Spray with miticides (e.g., abamectin) or insecticidal soap
Increase humidity (mites dislike moisture)
Release natural predators (predatory mites)
prevention: Maintain adequate watering and plant vigor
Monitor plants regularly, especially in hot dry weather

symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric ring pattern
Yellow halo around lesions
Affects leaves, stems, and fruit
Defoliation under severe conditions
causes: Dense planting with poor air circulation

diseases.details: Target Spot is present on this plant. Circular brown spots with lighter rings inside — like a target — appear on the leaves. The disease can spread to stems and fruit if not treated and causes significant leaf drop.

pests.details: Pests do not cause Target Spot. This is a fungal disease and is not spread by insects.

overwatering.details: Overwatering can contribute to Target Spot. Wet leaves and high humidity help the fungus grow and spread. Water only at the base of the plant — never wet the foliage.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Target Spot. The disease needs warm, moist conditions to spread — dry conditions are not a risk.

soil.details: Soil conditions can contribute to Target Spot. The fungus lives in old plant material in the soil. Remove all plant debris after harvest and rotate crops each season.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not cause Target Spot. Good sunlight and air circulation help keep leaves dry and reduce fungal spread.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Target Spot. Keep the plant well-fed to help it recover more quickly after treatment.

generalStress.details: This disease is putting stress on the plant. Significant leaf loss reduces tomato production. Remove infected leaves and spray with fungicide as soon as possible.

treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil or Azoxystrobin fungicide at first symptom
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Improve plant spacing for better air circulation
Spray every 7-10 days during humid conditions
prevention: Maintain adequate plant spacing — avoid dense planting
Stake and train plants for canopy ventilation
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Remove plant debris after harvest
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Target Spot is an issue affecting Tomato. Proper management is required.

symptoms: Yellowing and curling of young leaves
Stunted plant growth
Poor fruit set and yield
causes: Virus: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV)
No chemical cure, virus persists in plant
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus is a viral disease that causes yellow, curled leaves and severe stunting. Infected plants produce few fruit and yield is drastically reduced.

diseases.details: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus is present on this plant. Infected plants have yellowing and cupped young leaves, and overall growth is stunted. The virus is carried by whiteflies and there is no direct treatment.

pests.details: Pest damage is contributing to this problem. Whiteflies transmit the virus when they feed on the plant. Controlling whiteflies is critical to managing this disease.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause TYLCV. The disease is transmitted by whiteflies, not irrigation.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause TYLCV. However, drought-stressed plants may be more severely impacted by viral infection.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause TYLCV. The virus is systemic in the plant and spread by insects.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause TYLCV. Plants should still receive full sun for best growth and vigor.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause TYLCV. However, balanced fertilization helps keep plants healthier overall.

generalStress.details: TYLCV causes severe stress to the plant by reducing photosynthesis and growth. Infected plants often have no marketable yield.

treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly with appropriate insecticides
Use reflective mulches to repel whiteflies
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Use insect-proof screens in greenhouses
Apply reflective mulch and avoid planting near infested areas

expert_resources.title: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (UC IPM)

symptoms: Mottled, light and dark green mosaic patterns on leaves
Stunted and distorted plant growth
Reduced yield and fruit quality
causes: Virus: Tobacco mosaic virus strain infecting tomato
Spread by contaminated tools, hands, or seed
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Tomato Mosaic Virus is a viral disease that causes a green mosaic pattern on leaves and stunts plant growth. Infected plants produce smaller, often misshapen fruit.

diseases.details: Tomato Mosaic Virus is present on this plant. The virus causes mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves. Infected plants may be stunted with distorted foliage. Fruit can be small and reduced in quality. The virus spreads easily via contact.

pests.details: Pest damage can contribute to disease spread. While insects do not directly transmit ToMV, their movement on plants can spread virus particles. Wounds can also allow viral infection to worsen.

overwatering.details: Overwatering does not cause Tomato Mosaic Virus. The virus is spread by contact, not by irrigation.

underwatering.details: Underwatering does not cause Tomato Mosaic Virus. However, drought stress can exacerbate virus symptoms in the plant.

soil.details: Soil conditions do not cause Tomato Mosaic Virus. The virus is present in plant tissue and spread mechanically.

sunlight.details: Sunlight does not directly cause Tomato Mosaic Virus. Adequate sunlight remains important for healthy growth.

nutrientDeficiency.details: Nutrient deficiency does not cause Tomato Mosaic Virus. However, well-nourished plants may cope better with infection.

generalStress.details: Tomato Mosaic Virus stresses the plant by disrupting normal growth and reducing fruit set. Plants may remain stunted and yield lower harvests.

treatment: No chemical control; remove infected plants
Disinfect tools and hands to prevent spread
Use virus-free seed and resistant varieties
prevention: Practice good hygiene (clean tools/hands)
Rotate crops to break virus cycles

diseases.details: No disease detected. Your tomato plant looks healthy and the leaves look clean.

pests.details: No pest damage visible. Check under leaves regularly for small insects like whiteflies or spider mites.

overwatering.details: No signs of overwatering. Continue watering at the base of the plant to keep roots healthy and leaves dry.

underwatering.details: No signs of underwatering. The plant looks well-watered and is growing normally.

soil.details: Soil conditions look fine. Rotate crops each season to prevent soil-borne diseases from building up.

sunlight.details: The plant is getting enough sunlight. Tomatoes need full sun for good fruit production — continue as normal.

nutrientDeficiency.details: No signs of nutrient shortage. Keep fertilizing regularly through the growing season.

generalStress.details: The plant is not under any stress. Monitor every 2-3 days to catch any early signs of disease or pest activity.

prevention: Apply preventive fungicide during cool wet weather for Late Blight protection
Maintain consistent N nutrition throughout season
Install drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Scout daily for whitefly and aphid populations
diseases.doctor_diagnosis: Healthy is an issue affecting Tomato. Proper management is required.
